sentences.text: At the ghetto, the only thing that I can remember were little anecdotes.
Because I can sit here with you for-- for three weeks and tell you the stories.
But after the evacuation of my parents, three days later, when I found out that everybody is dead, we took to the bottle.
We had--
Liquor was always available.
Liquor was available in camp.
Was from Pollocks, which they brought it in on black market.
They used to bring us liquor by the [INAUDIBLE] wagon.
And they used to take out clothing.
So we use the exchange.
Sometimes we'll pay them additional cash, but we never ran out of liquor.
Our period of over two years, I don't think there was a day that we should-- shouldn't have any liquor.
And drinking-- I lived with five more people than-- except me-- we used to drink every day, at least [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fifths.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had-- by breakfast, we used to have a fifth each one.
It was just like drinking water.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And money we had.
I mean, I had, and my group.
But my brothers were there.
They didn't have any money, but they had enough.
It's like when the evacuation was from the whole ghetto, my friend, which lived beneath the ghetto, they put it a chief of staff of the camp.
They gave my brothers some jobs.
They should go in and pick out the clothing.
Now, in the clothing, they found a lot of jewelry and all kind of-- all kind of money.
That's-- they had enough money, too.
But most of the people which worked in the factories, they didn't have no money.
We had a kitchen in camp.
They used to go for breakfast and for dinner.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when we started to drink, every night and every morning, we did-- we never-- we never quit until we were evacuated to, to Auschwitz.
We were taken by train by open cars.
We knew it the day before, that we were-- we, we will be taken to Auschwitz.
But that day before, they, they put a ring around the camp so that nobody could escape.
So we had to abide by going to Auschwitz.
On the way to Auschwitz, I had some friends which we broke a hole through in the car, the freight car.
And they slipped through, but I don't know what happened to them.
I never met them.
I've never seen them again.
I remember, but now it's-- in a minute, it's just out of my mind.
Yeah, we considered too.
I was there with my, my two brothers.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think it was that was enough time for all three of us to go through.
But then later, we abandoned that and we decided to go with everybody.
That journey was a whole day.
On the train, there was-- there was no conditions.
We didn't get anything.
We, we didn't need anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Whoever was smart, took along with them some bread or something to drink.
But most people had nothing with them.
When we came to Auschwitz, we undressed, and we went into Auschwitz without anything on our-- on our body.
We got the numbers.
When we arrived to Auschwitz, we went to the-- to the bath house.
We took a bath and they introduced-- intro-- introduced-- they looked if anybody brought with them gold or any valuables with them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After this, we took a shower because usually when-- we knew it, when people take a shower, they get gassed.
But at that time, they needed this 800 people-- from both camps, together 1600, they needed to work.
They had at that time-- it was already in, in the end of '44-- they needed somebody to work because they killed most of them off.
So they needed somebody to replace.
And they couldn't get their people in, so they left us to take us to, to factories.
No, didn't-- I wasn't aware of anything.
They only thing I was-- I was-- I wasn't even worried.
I went in.
That was the end.
That's right.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After we went out from the showers, and they started to put our numbers on.
That was in late '45-- '44 I mean.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After they gave us the numbers, we knew.
We were already worried that they-- they were going to terminate us, but we knew that we are going to go out for-- in another factory or wherever it is to work.
So I went with my oldest brother to Buna, no Dora.
That was a-- that was a working camp.
We had there about 28,000 people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at the time, I registered as a carpenter, and my brother tested, too.
So my younger brother, he didn't have any.
He-- whatever-- whatever he wanted to give in-- so he'd tell them I'm a carpenter, I'm a shoe maker, whatever he wanted.
Whatever they needed, anybody.
So when we went, I went with my older brother together.
And my younger brother went to [?
Szebnie. ?] That was about 12 kilometers away from my wife used to live.
And you and your older brother-- this would have been Meyer?
Abram.
That's my oldest-- my oldest brother.
He used to-- he was alive until the day after liberation.
In Buna.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were in Buna from [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] August '44 until January '45.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, we were evacuated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and went to Buchenwald.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to Buchenwald, and we stayed in Buchenwald for three days.
By train.
We were walking to Gleiwitz.
That's 32 miles by-- in snow by foot-- on foot.
And from-- from Gleiwitz, we, we took-- we had the train waiting.
We went into the train and for nine days and nine nights, we were traveling to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Buchenwald to Halberstadt.
But we never arrived to Halberstadt.
We stayed on the-- We stayed on the train, and we never arrived to Halberstadt.
We were on the way to-- the train was going, from Gleiwitz to Buchenwald.
No, not from Gleiwitz.
From Buchenwald to Halberstadt.
And on the way, we were strafed by-- by planes, by English planes.
They were shooting down.
Like, we were on higher embankment, and they were lower.
They went down very low with the planes, and they strafed us.
They had the big 5-inch bullets.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were 1,000 people on that train, plus we were-- we had about 80 SS.
In our car, we were about 50 [GERMAN] another thing in camp, and there were four SS with us in the car.
We shouldn't break out or so, whatever it is.
When they were shooting at the train, they killed the four-- the four SS men, including maybe 30 Jews.
I caught two bullets-- one bullet in my leg and one bullet in my back.
I was taken off in North Hausen.
So they, they, they took me.
They threw me down from the-- from the-- from the truck, and they took me to the [INAUDIBLE] Hospital.
Why?
They needed the workers at that time.
They couldn't leave you to die in there out on the field.
After they shot-- they shot us, everybody who was alive, was able to run, jumped down from the-- from the train and was running out to the field.
But I could only run a short period of time, and then I had to sit down, because I couldn't-- I was shot in my-- in my leg and my back.
So I remained there until they picked me up, and they put me on the truck with all the dead people.
I was on top.
And they took me to the camp.
I came-- when we came into the camp, they took me off from the top.
They took me to the hospital.
And I remained at-- there.
The first three weeks, I had a bullet in my leg and they didn't even know that I have a bullet in my leg.
And from the bullet I got a gangrene.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So in about, about three weeks later on, there came in a doctor, a Polish doctor from our city.
It was just plain luck.
Dr.
Pollock.
He came in and they took me in to see what happening with my leg.
And the-- and the back where I caught the bullet, it just striped me.
It tore out a piece of flesh, but that healed up right away.
So when he came in and he took me down to the operating room, where they didn't have anything to do-- to do or to give you.
And he-- that's all he had, was a scalpel.
He said, I can't do nothing with you.
I have to take off the leg.
Maybe you'll survive.
I said I didn't want it taken off, the leg.
If you take off the leg, in the next hour or next two hours, I'll be dead.
So while I'm here, if I, if I have to die, I want to die whole.
I don't want to die half.
So he start to ask me questions from where I am and what my name is.
So it turned out that he was from our city, and he knew my father very good.
He was-- he was playing billiards with them in Hotel Polski.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] at first, he didn't know what to do with me.
So he called the boys in, he said, help us, and they should bring me back to their-- to the barrack.
They put me into the barrack.
I was in the barrack maybe an hour, an hour and a half.
They came back.
The doctor wants to see you again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I go with them.
I didn't go, there were carrying me.
I come in.
He says, look.
I knew your father very good.
I played with him billiard.
And he told me what kind of business my father was and what he did.
He said, I want to do something for you, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but you have to let me do it.
I'll cut away whatever I can.
So maybe it's going to help you.
You don't have to lose your leg.
And maybe-- I don't say I will or I won't, but I'll try.
But one thing, I haven't got any anesthetics or anything to give you.
He said one thing I can give you is a hunk of paper, toilet paper.
You should take it between your teeth, you shouldn't break your teeth.
And that's how we did.
He put me on the-- they put me on the table.
And he started operation.
For four hours, he was cutting and scraping.
After he finished, he put the toilet paper around my leg and he had a-- a-- a drainer, like a gutter.
He said now, you have to pray to God.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everything should be OK, because I can't do anymore for you.
I did the best what I could.
And they took me back into the barrack.
I stayed for seven days.
They put me in the back and they put a pail of-- an empty pail at the front of the bed.
And they put my leg right-- I should-- where it's dripping.
It should drip into the pail.
I was laying for eight days like this.
After eight days, he came in-- the doctor-- he says let's go-- let's go-- let's go in.
So his boys took me into the operating room.
He cut away the, the bandages whatever I had.
And he opened up.

He says, I can't see anything, but the one thing we have to do more, we have to put drainers in-- in your leg-- not only at the part where you were shot, but we have to put from the bottom to the top.
But that's going to hurt.
I said to him, so it's going to hurt.
Please?
My leg healed, yes.
My leg-- I-- about two and half months later, I went back-- I went to Poland, home, where maybe I--
That was-- well, this time, I was liberated, when I was at the-- when I was-- right after the operation.
That was in April, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the beginning of April.
The 5th, [INAUDIBLE] was liberated.
In March, I, I was in the hospital, and he was treating me.
I had 27 operations on my leg until then.
No.
No, I wasn't liberated at the time, yet.
I was still-- I was still at the-- the Germans were still in the camp.
They were still there.
But I was on the end of March, they started to put together groups and take them deep into Germany.
Now, the, the, the-- a kapo-- you know what a kapo is?
He used to-- he was-- he came into the-- to the hospital.
He said, anybody who can walk, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they should-- tomorrow morning should go with the group.
Everybody's got to go.
So I said to him, and people who cannot walk.
He says, you have to remain.
I said, what's going to happen to those people.
He said, I can only tell you what I hear.
I hear they're going to take your men to the tunnel, because there, there they were making the V1, V2.
And they'll explode the whole tunnel, including the people.
I said, what can I do.
Where will I go?
The first ten steps, they're going to kill me anyway.
So I remained.
In the afternoon, came in a general from the German army.
And he said, nobody's getting touched.
Everything remains the way it is.
They brought doctors in, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] because two days later, the English came in.
They capitulated, and they gave everything over to the English.
In that area was the English army.
So when the English came in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had doctors.
They used to come in every morning and go home
00, 5:00 in the afternoon.
When I was liberated, when the English came in.
How I felt?
A matter of fact, I didn't feel any difference.
The only difference I felt is maybe I can see my brothers, because I knew my family, the rest of the family, they're all gone.
So I figured maybe I can find my brothers still alive, somebody.
But I was in camp there for six or seven weeks after the liberation.
I was in the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we decided to go to-- home, to Kielce.
Maybe we'll find somebody there.
So when we when we-- we were going on a train.
So every 10 miles, another Russian captain or general took away the, the locomotive, and we were stuck.
We were staying.
It took us about eight or nine days to go from there to a city in Poland.
And between, between, in that time, there were girls.
There were some Russian from the army, soldiers.
They used to go after the girls.
They raped them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was a general on the train, and he himself shot three Russian soldiers for raping the girls.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He didn't want to have no court, no nothing.
He just plain took out the Browning and shot them.
When I arrived to-- in Kielce, first thing, I didn't go to my house.
First thing, I went to my uncle's.
And I knew-- when I came in, I was told that my uncle lives there and there.
So I went to him, and he told me what, what-- he was liberated in January.
He's dead now-- Joe [INAUDIBLE].
My-- he was my, my mother's brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you need a doctor.
He took me to a doctor.
He told me the story that my younger brother was hid there and was waiting I should come.
But he couldn't wait any longer.
He wanted to get-- to go out of Poland.
So him and some friends of his, they went to Italy, because in Italy, the Catholics, the Catholic Church-- if you went in and you said you want to convert, you used to get paid money.
We'll go to break now.
